condition.qualitative_description: Forest area (ha) has declined over the 2017–2024 period, indicating net loss of tree cover in the micro-watershed or landscape unit.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A measurable decline in forest area directly reduces the habitat of NTFP-bearing trees and understory species. In the semi-arid Deccan Plateau (AER-6), dry deciduous forests supporting species such as Madhuca indica (mahua), Terminalia chebula (harra), and Buchanania lanzan (achar) are particularly sensitive to canopy loss. Reduced canopy cover also degrades microclimate conditions required by shade-tolerant understory NTFP species. Yadav and Dugaya (2013) identify these species as central to NTFP livelihoods in central India, and their availability is directly contingent on intact forest cover.

condition.qualitative_description: Cumulative deforestation exceeds 30 ha or forest-to-farm conversion exceeds 25 ha, indicating substantial habitat loss for NTFP species even when net canopy trend is flat.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Cumulative deforestation quantifies the total extent of habitat loss for NTFP-bearing species. In semi-arid regions with naturally sparse forest cover, even moderate deforestation (>50 ha) can eliminate critical patches of dry deciduous forest that support the bulk of NTFP collection. Shankar et al. (2004) document that NTFP dependence in forest-fringe communities in south India is tightly coupled to the ecological integrity of remaining forest patches, and that degradation shifts outcomes from 'win-win' to 'lose-lose' for both livelihoods and biodiversity.

condition.qualitative_description: Afforestation is less than 35% of cumulative deforestation, indicating weak forest recovery relative to habitat loss (amplifying signal only).

diagnostic_signals.explanation: When afforestation investment is small relative to deforestation, remaining NTFP-bearing forest is exposed to continued degradation pressures (grazing, fuelwood extraction, encroachment). Community forest resource rights under FRA and Joint Forest Management are critical mechanisms for protecting residual NTFP habitat; low afforestation relative to loss indicates these governance and recovery mechanisms are absent or weak.

condition.expression: organization_domains does not include any of ['NTFP', 'agroforestry', 'community forestry', 'forest rights', 'JFM']
condition.qualitative_description: No NGO or civil society organisation active in NTFP support, agroforestry, or forest rights domains is present in the area, indicating absence of institutional support for NTFP-dependent communities.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The presence of organisations working in NTFP or community forestry domains is associated with better forest governance outcomes, including JFM committees, CFR rights claims under FRA, and sustainable harvesting practices. Their absence means communities lack facilitation for collective action to protect forests. Yadav and Dugaya (2013) note that institutional frameworks and market organisation are key determinants of whether NTFP harvesting remains ecologically sustainable. Absence of such organisations amplifies degradation risk by leaving governance gaps unfilled.

overall_reasoning_note: Forest degradation as the driver of NTFP decline should be confirmed by at least one primary signal: substantial cumulative deforestation or forest-to-farm conversion (sig_02), and/or a declining lulc_tree_forest_ha trend (sig_01). Large gross deforestation from change detection can confirm the pathway even when net canopy cover is stable or noisy year-to-year. The afforestation ratio (sig_03) and absence of institutional support (sig_04) are amplifying signals only.The cd_total_afforestation_ha ratio (sig_03) and absence of supporting organisations (sig_04) are amplifying signals that indicate vulnerability to continued decline rather than confirming the degradation pathway on their own. The most critical missing variable is direct survey data on NTFP species presence and abundance (ntfp_species_presence), which would allow confirmation that forest loss has translated into reduced NTFP availability on the ground. In semi-arid Deccan Plateau contexts, forest patches are naturally fragmented and small; even modest deforestation can eliminate functionally important patches. Always ask communities about changes in collection effort, travel distance to collection sites, and species availability before finalising diagnosis.

confounders.confounder: Over-harvesting of NTFPs by communities causing species depletion independent of forest cover loss
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If lulc_tree_forest_ha shows stable or increasing forest cover but NTFP availability is declining, over-harvesting rather than forest degradation is the primary driver. Shankar et al. (2004) document that high dependence on NTFPs combined with poor market organisation and low indigenous ecological knowledge leads to ecological costs even in intact forests. Ask communities whether collection effort has increased (more collectors, longer hours) while yields have fallen — this pattern indicates over-harvesting rather than habitat loss.

confounders.confounder: Seasonal or inter-annual rainfall variability causing temporary NTFP yield decline unrelated to structural forest degradation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: In semi-arid AER-6 contexts with 500–960 mm rainfall, drought years can suppress flowering and fruiting of NTFP species (e.g., mahua, aonla) without any change in forest cover. If cd_total_deforestation_ha is low and lulc_tree_forest_ha is stable, but NTFP yields have declined in recent years coinciding with below-normal rainfall, drought is the more likely proximate cause. Compare NTFP decline years against rainfall records; if decline is episodic and correlated with drought, forest degradation is not the primary pathway.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have community members noticed a reduction in the number or density of key NTFP-producing trees or plants in the forest — for example, mahua (Madhuca indica), tendu (Diospyros melanoxylon), harra (Terminalia chebula), or achar (Buchanania lanzan)? Would you say these species are: (a) as abundant as 10 years ago, (b) noticeably fewer or harder to find, or (c) largely absent from areas where they used to grow?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If communities report option (b) or (c), this directly confirms that forest degradation has translated into reduced NTFP species abundance on the ground, strongly confirming the forest_degradation pathway. If species are still present but yields are lower, over-harvesting or rainfall variability may be the primary driver rather than structural forest loss, shifting the diagnosis toward alternative pathways.

sources.title: Potential for Recognition of Community Forest Resource Rights Under India's Forest Rights Act
sources.relevance_note: Documents the FRA framework for CFR rights covering at least 40 million hectares across 170,000 villages; supports recommendation for JFM/CFR rights as a solution to forest degradation affecting NTFP-dependent communities

sources.title: Livelihood gains and ecological costs of non-timber forest product dependence: assessing the roles of dependence, ecological knowledge and market organisation
sources.relevance_note: Examines NTFP dependence at three south India sites; documents that ecological degradation of forest patches shifts outcomes from win-win to lose-lose for both livelihoods and biodiversity; identifies dependence level, indigenous ecological knowledge, and market organisation as key factors

sources.title: Non-timber forest products certification in India: opportunities and challenges
sources.relevance_note: Documents socio-economic and cultural importance of NTFPs for forest-dwelling communities in India; lists key NTFP species (tendu, harra, achar, aonla, mahua) relevant to central and peninsular India; highlights that collection methods can be destructive and wild populations are declining

sources.title: Sustainable Forest Management Beyond the Timber-Oriented Status Quo: Transitioning to Co-production of Timber and Non-woody Forest Products
sources.relevance_note: Reviews global evidence that NTFP availability is contingent on forest governance and management regimes; documents that policy and property rights affect sustainability of NTFP resources; supports transition from command-and-control to community governance as a solution

sources.title: The Role of Non-Timber Forest Products (NTFPs) in Tribal Economy of Jharkhand, India
sources.relevance_note: Quantifies NTFP contribution to tribal household income (17.18% of gross annual income); documents species diversity and seasonal patterns of NTFP exploitation; provides baseline for understanding livelihood stakes of NTFP decline
